explanation: The requirement states that in Standby Mode, the user must be able to adjust all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms (before the patient is connected and before starting ventilation). The atomic proposition 'StandbyMode' indicates that the system is in Standby Mode, and 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' and 'alarmSettingsChanged' capture the ability to adjust ventilation parameters and alarm settings. We encode the requirement that whenever the system is in Standby Mode, both ventilation and alarm parameters are adjustable (possibly repeatedly). This can be captured by stating that, globally, if StandbyMode holds, then both ventilationParamsAdjustable and alarmSettingsChanged should always be possible. Therefore, we use the template for 'Globally, if condition then property always holds.'

The requirement states that in Standby Mode, all ventilation and alarm parameters that are user-controlled must be adjustable before connecting to the patient and before ventilation starts. The atomic proposition 'StandbyMode' represents the Standby Mode, and 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' and 'alarmSettingsChanged' indicate that ventilation parameters and alarm settings can be adjusted, respectively. 'patientConnected' means the patient has been connected, and 'ventilating' means ventilation has started. We want to ensure that, at all times in Standby Mode before the patient is connected and before ventilation starts, it is always possible to adjust these parameters.

This is correctly expressed by G ( (StandbyMode & (! patientConnected) & (! ventilating)) -> (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged) ), which states that globally, whenever we are in Standby Mode and the patient is not connected and the system is not ventilating, it is possible to adjust both ventilation and alarm parameters. This ensures the system property as required.

The natural language specifies that when the ventilator is in Standby Mode (that is, ventilation is off and parameters can be set before connecting the patient), all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms must be adjustable. The atomic proposition 'StandbyMode' represents being in Standby Mode, and 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' represents that ventilation parameters are adjustable. To encode that, whenever the system is in Standby Mode, ventilation parameters must be adjustable (i.e., it is always the case that if Standby Mode is active, then ventilation parameters are adjustable). This is best captured with the 'globally implies' template: G (StandbyMode -> ventilationParamsAdjustable). This template ensures the property is always enforced as long as the system is in Standby Mode.

The natural language requirement specifies that in Standby Mode, before the patient is connected and ventilation is started, it must be possible to adjust all user-controlled parameters for both ventilation and alarms. In the provided atomic propositions, 'StandbyMode' indicates the system is in Standby Mode, 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' indicates ventilation parameters are adjustable, and 'alarmSettingsChanged' indicates that alarm settings can be changed (or at least, the action of changing alarm settings). The requirement thus translates to: whenever the system is in Standby Mode, ventilation parameters must be adjustable and the user must be able to change alarm settings. Since both ventilation parameter adjustability and alarm setting adjustment are needed, we use the AND operator. The most appropriate template is 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)', i.e., globally, if Standby Mode is active, then it shall be possible to adjust all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms. Here, bool_exp1 = 'StandbyMode' and bool_exp3 = 'ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged'. This LTL formula thus directly encodes the requirement that in Standby Mode, all these user adjustments are available at all times.

The requirement states that in Standby Mode, all ventilation and alarm parameters that can be adjusted by the user must be adjustable before connecting to the patient and starting ventilation. The atomic proposition 'StandbyMode' captures the Standby Mode context, 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' expresses that ventilation parameters can be adjusted, and 'alarmSettingsChanged' covers user-controlled alarm adjustments. The property uses G (globally) to specify that whenever the system is in Standby Mode and the patient is not yet connected, it must be possible for both ventilation and alarm parameters to be adjusted. Furthermore, as adjustment is a permission, this is a safety-style property requiring these things hold as long as Standby Mode holds. Thus, we use a conjunction of being in StandbyMode and not patientConnected triggering (->) that ventilation and alarm parameters are adjustable. N_DURATION is not needed for this template. (Template 30).

The requirement is that, when in Standby Mode and before connecting a patient, it should be possible to adjust all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms. Translating this with available atomic propositions: 'StandbyMode' denotes the mode, 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' means ventilation parameters can be set, and 'alarmSettingsChanged' signals that the alarm settings were changed (which, as a proposition, can also be interpreted as being able to adjust alarms). Thus, while in StandbyMode and before the patient is connected, both parameters must be adjustable. This can be captured as: whenever (StandbyMode AND NOT patientConnected) holds, (ventilationParamsAdjustable AND alarmSettingsChanged) must hold as well. This is a simple implication across the system, so the suitable template is 40.

To fit the template's strict boolean logic, bool_exp3 is (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged).

The requirement specifies that while the system is in Standby Mode (ventilation off and ready to ventilate), it must be possible to adjust all user-controlled ventilation and alarm parameters before connecting to the patient and beginning ventilation. The atomic propositions relevant here are 'StandbyMode', 'ventilationParamsAdjustable', and 'alarmSettingsChanged'. The 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' and 'alarmSettingsChanged' atomic propositions cover that all ventilation/alarm parameters are user-adjustable. The guarantee must hold globally while in StandbyMode. Thus, the property is: whenever the system is in Standby Mode, all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms can be adjusted. The most relevant template is a safety invariant: 'G (StandbyMode -> (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged))' (template 30), with N_DURATION null since there is no bounded time required.

The requirement states that while in Standby Mode, before connecting the patient and starting ventilation, all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms must be adjustable. In the given atomic propositions, "StandbyMode" indicates that the system is in Standby, and "ventilationParamsAdjustable" and "alarmSettingsChanged" best represent the idea of being able to adjust ventilation and alarm parameters. To ensure that all user-controlled parameters (ventilation and alarms) can be set, we use: StandbyMode implies that ventilation parameters are adjustable and that alarm settings can be changed. This is captured by:

G (StandbyMode -> (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged))

This means: 'Globally, whenever the system is in Standby Mode, it shall be possible to adjust both ventilation and alarm parameters.'

- chosen_template_ID: 30 (which encodes G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3))
- bool_exp1: StandbyMode
- bool_exp3: (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged)
- N_DURATION: null (not used in this template)

The input requirement states that when the system is in Standby Mode (i.e., StandbyMode is true), the user must be able to adjust all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms prior to connecting the patient and starting ventilation. In the context of the given atomic propositions, we interpret "user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms" as being represented by 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' for ventilation parameters and 'alarmSettingsChanged' for alarm parameters. Therefore, the property that should hold is: whenever the system is in Standby Mode, the ventilation parameters must be adjustable and the alarm settings can change (i.e., the user has access to them). This is expressed as 'StandbyMode -> (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged)' globally (at all times), capturing the idea that in Standby Mode, adjustment of both sets of parameters is possible.

The natural language states that in Standby Mode (i.e., before patient is connected and ventilation starts), it must be possible to adjust all user-controlled parameters for ventilation and alarms. In the provided atomic propositions, 'StandbyMode' indicates the system is in Standby Mode, while 'ventilationParamsAdjustable' and 'alarmSettingsChanged' refer to the ability to adjust ventilation parameters and that alarm settings can be changed. Therefore, in all states where 'StandbyMode' is true, it should always be possible to adjust ventilation parameters and change alarm settings—captured by 'ventilationParamsAdjustable | alarmSettingsChanged'. This requirement must always hold while in StandbyMode. Thus, template 17 ('G (bool_exp2 -> (G bool_exp3))') is appropriate, with bool_exp2 = StandbyMode and bool_exp3 = (ventilationParamsAdjustable | alarmSettingsChanged).
